raw_branches.text: Cohort A (standard outcome OKR) week 3 retention drops to 36% (18/50) vs Cohort B (habit cue OKR) which stays at 66% (33/50).
Cohort C (tool reminders) achieves 38% week 3 retention (19/50), showing reminders do not improve outcome retention compared to standard outcome OKRs (Cohort A).
Trigger-action cues and review scheduling are strongly adopted in the successful Cohort B.

deduped_branches.text: Cohort A (standard outcome goals) drops to 36% week 3 retention (n=50, Mar-Apr), whereas Cohort B (habit cue goals) maintains 66% retention (n=50).
Cohort C (tool reminders) achieves only 38% week 3 retention (n=50), indicating that push notifications do not bypass the habit constraint.
Writing explicit trigger-action cues (82% in Cohort B vs 10% in Cohort A) strongly correlates with increased OKR retention.

load_bearing_sent_to_refute.text: Cohort C (tool reminders) achieves only 38% week 3 retention (n=50), indicating that push notifications do not bypass the habit constraint.

refutations.evidence: Cohort C retention (38%) matches Cohort A baseline (36%), refuting the hypothesis that software tool reminders are the primary lever for habit automation.
